The **Hunton Megagroup** also **Hunton Super Group, Hunton Group, Hunton Formation** and **Hunton Limestone** is predominantly composed of [carbonate rock](/wiki/Carbonate_rock "Carbonate rock"), deposited between the [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") and early to mid [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian") periods. In many States it acts as a reservoir for both hydrocarbons and water.[[2]](#cite_note-2)

## Stratigraphy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunton_Megagroup&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Stratigraphy")]

### Clifty Limestone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunton_Megagroup&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Clifty Limestone")]

The **Clifty Limestone** is a Middle [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian") [geologic formation](/wiki/Formation_(geology) "Formation (geology)") in the [Ozark Plateaus](/wiki/Ozark_Plateau "Ozark Plateau") of [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"). This thin formation can but up to 4 feet thick.[[3]](#cite_note-McFarland-3) The name was introduced in 1916 by Albert Homer Purdue and Hugh Dinsmore Miser in their study of northern Arkansas. They designated a [stratotype](/wiki/Stratotype "Stratotype") along the East Fork of Little Clifty Creek in [Benton County](/wiki/Benton_County,_Arkansas "Benton County, Arkansas"), Arkansas. The Clifty Limestone is very sandy, light blue to bluish grey.[[4]](#cite_note-4)

### Penters Chert

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunton_Megagroup&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Penters Chert")]

The **Penters Chert** is a [Devonian](/wiki/Devonian "Devonian") [geologic formation](/wiki/Formation_(geology) "Formation (geology)") in the [Ozark Plateaus](/wiki/Ozark_Plateau "Ozark Plateau") of [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"). Its thickness ranges up to 90'.[[3]](#cite_note-McFarland-3) The name was introduced in 1921 by Hugh Dinsmore Miser in his study of Arkansas.[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) Miser designated a type locality near the old Penters Bluff railroad station in [Izard County](/wiki/Izard_County,_Arkansas "Izard County, Arkansas"), Arkansas, however, he did not assign a [stratotype](/wiki/Stratotype "Stratotype"). As of 2017, a reference section has not been designated for this unit.

### Bailey Limestone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunton_Megagroup&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Bailey Limestone")]

The Bailey Limestone was first described by Ulrich, Buckley and Buehler, 1904, p. 110. It was named for the now abandoned town of Bailey's Landing near the village of Grand Eddy, on the [Mississippi River](/wiki/Mississippi_River "Mississippi River"). The formation is gray to greenish gray, silty with chert inclusions, thin-bedded, very hard limestone.[[6]](#cite_note-6)

### Lafferty Limestone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunton_Megagroup&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: Lafferty Limestone")]

The **Lafferty Limestone** is a Middle to Late [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") [geologic formation](/wiki/Formation_(geology) "Formation (geology)") in the [Ozark Plateaus](/wiki/Ozark_Plateau "Ozark Plateau") of [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas").[[5]](#cite_note-:0-5) The name was introduced in 1921 by Hugh Dinsmore Miser in his study of Arkansas, replacing part of the upper [St. Clair Limestone](/wiki/St._Clair_Limestone "St. Clair Limestone").[[3]](#cite_note-McFarland-3) Miser designated a type locality at Tate Spring, located 1.25 miles north of the site of the old Penters Bluff railroad station in [Izard County](/wiki/Izard_County,_Arkansas "Izard County, Arkansas"), Arkansas, however, he did not assign a [stratotype](/wiki/Stratotype "Stratotype"). As of 2017, a reference section has not been designated for this unit.

### Racine Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunton_Megagroup&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Racine Formation")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8d/Glyptambon_gassi_Edgecombe%2C_1991.jpg/250px-Glyptambon_gassi_Edgecombe%2C_1991.jpg)](/wiki/File:Glyptambon_gassi_Edgecombe,_1991.jpg)

Glyptambon gassi of Racine Dolomite Formation, Illinois, Edgecombe, 1991

The **Racine Formation** or **Racine** **Dolomite** is found from [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin") south through [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") and [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"). Its age is from 423 to 427 million years old. It contains fossils of many species of [trilobites](/wiki/Trilobite "Trilobite"), [brachiopods](/wiki/Brachiopod "Brachiopod"), [crinoids](/wiki/Crinoid "Crinoid"), [gastropods](/wiki/Gastropoda "Gastropoda") among others[[8]](#cite_note-8) This formation is found in [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), [Iowa](/wiki/Iowa "Iowa"), [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri") and [Wisconsin](/wiki/Wisconsin "Wisconsin"). The Racine is estimated to contain over 7 million barrels of oil in Illinois.[[9]](#cite_note-9)

### Moccasin Springs Formation

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunton_Megagroup&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Moccasin Springs Formation")]

The **Moccasin Springs Formation** is found in [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois") and [Kentucky](/wiki/Kentucky "Kentucky"). It is also recognized in [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana").[[10]](#cite_note-10) This formation was first described by Becker (1974). The Moccasin Springs contains [reef](/wiki/Reef "Reef") facies that are more common at its base. These are pure [calcium carbonate](/wiki/Calcium_carbonate "Calcium carbonate") ([limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone")). These reefs also make up the [Terre Haute Bank](/w/index.php?title=Terre_Haute_Bank&action=edit&redlink=1 "Terre Haute Bank (page does not exist)") on the eastern flank of the [Illinois Basin](/wiki/Illinois_Basin "Illinois Basin"). The top of the formation is mostly dark-gray to black dolomitic shale. This is occasionally interbedded very fine grained limestone, that is mostly dark-greenish-gray. Below is dense to fine grained limestone. It tends to be somewhat argillaceous. The colors range from pastel shades of pink, green, yellow, tan, and gray to saturated dark-gray and red to purple colors. These colors may be mottled as well.[[11]](#cite_note-11)

### St. Clair Limestone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunton_Megagroup&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: St. Clair Limestone")]

The **St. Clair Limestone** is a geologic unit in [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas"), and Oklahoma. It is classified as a Geologic Member in Indiana and Missouri. It dates back to the Middle of [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") [period](/wiki/Period_(geology) "Period (geology)"). It is high density, high magnesium dolomitic limestone.[[12]](#cite_note-12) It was originally classified as a marble in Oklahoma due to the fact that it would hold a high polish, hence [Marble City](/wiki/Marble_City,_Oklahoma "Marble City, Oklahoma"). It is sold in slabs and as tiles, in a similar manner as marble would be.[[13]](#cite_note-13) This unit has many economic uses in Arkansas and Oklahoma. It is used as a construction material, manufacture of [quicklime](/wiki/Quicklime "Quicklime"), and manganese deposits are mined as well.[[14]](#cite_note-14) The St Clair is designated as a member of the [Bainbridge Group](/wiki/Bainbridge_Group "Bainbridge Group") in the State of [Indiana](/wiki/Indiana "Indiana").[[15]](#cite_note-15) In [Illinois](/wiki/Illinois "Illinois"), [Missouri](/wiki/Missouri "Missouri"), [Arkansas](/wiki/Arkansas "Arkansas") and [Oklahoma](/wiki/Oklahoma "Oklahoma") it is part of the Hunton Megagroup. The St. Clair is the Basal member of the [Niagaran Series](/w/index.php?title=Niagaran_Series&action=edit&redlink=1 "Niagaran Series (page does not exist)"), making it part of the [Tippecanoe sequence](/wiki/Tippecanoe_sequence "Tippecanoe sequence"). Throughout most of the Southern extent the unit is roughly 10 to 20' thick. Moving northward it thickens to approximately 80 to 100' thick in the [Illinois Basin](/wiki/Illinois_Basin "Illinois Basin"). At its northernmost reaches where it grades in to the [Joliet](/w/index.php?title=Joliet_Formation&action=edit&redlink=1 "Joliet Formation (page does not exist)") and [Racine Formations](/wiki/Racine_Formation "Racine Formation") it is about 150' thick.

The St. Clair is composed of course calcite grains to fine grains. It may contain partings of claystone or [Mudstone](/wiki/Mudstone "Mudstone"). It can range in color from light-gray to chocolate brown, or even or purplish-black. Beds of pink [crinoid](/wiki/Crinoid "Crinoid") remains are also found with in this unit, mostly toward the base.

### Sexton Creek Limestone

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Hunton_Megagroup&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Sexton Creek Limestone")]

The **Sexton Creek Limestone** is [Silurian](/wiki/Silurian "Silurian") in age 443 - 441 Ma.[[16]](#cite_note-16) Named by (Savage, 1909, p. 518) revised by (Ulrich, 1911). It was named for Sexton Creek in [Alexander County, Illinois](/wiki/Alexander_County,_Illinois "Alexander County, Illinois"). The Sexton Creek is composed of [Limestone](/wiki/Limestone "Limestone"), [Dolomite](/wiki/Dolomite_(rock) "Dolomite (rock)") and [Chert](/wiki/Chert "Chert"). In parts of the unit there may be as much as 60% chert. In Indiana the Sexton Creek unconformably overlies [Ordovician](/wiki/Ordovician "Ordovician") aged rock. Further west it overlies the Edgewood Formation.[[17]](#cite_note-17)

In exposures the following fossils have been found; [Distacodus obliquicostatus](/w/index.php?title=Distacodus_obliquicostatus&action=edit&redlink=1 "Distacodus obliquicostatus (page does not exist)"), [Pterospathodus amorphognathoides](/wiki/Pterospathodus_amorphognathoides "Pterospathodus amorphognathoides"), [Kockelella ranuliformis](/w/index.php?title=Kockelella_ranuliformis&action=edit&redlink=1 "Kockelella ranuliformis (page does not exist)").
